metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix AC #3 to match the canonical prompt filename: replace `reviewers/enrich.md` with `reviewers/ticket_digest.md` so AC #3 and the Scope/Testing sections agree.
findings.finding: AC #3 names the wrong prompt file: it says `reviewers/enrich.md` but every other plan reference — Scope, Prompt section, test descriptions — uses `reviewers/ticket_digest.md`. An executing agent would create `ticket_digest.md` (per Scope) but write `test_prompt_excludes_logs` to open `enrich.md`, causing the test to fail on a missing file.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria bullet 3: 'The packaged `reviewers/enrich.md` prompt encodes the logs/stack-traces/code exclusion directive'
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC #3 to: 'The packaged `reviewers/ticket_digest.md` prompt (id `ticket-digest`) encodes the logs/stack-traces/code exclusion directive; `pytest tests/unit/test_enrich.py::test_prompt_excludes_logs`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix AC bullet 3 to reference the correct prompt file path `reviewers/ticket_digest.md` (not `reviewers/enrich.md`) so it matches the Scope, What, and AC bullet 6 sections.
findings.finding: AC bullet 3 names the prompt file as `reviewers/enrich.md`, but every other section in the plan (Scope, What/System prompt assembly, and AC bullet 6) names it `reviewers/ticket_digest.md`. An implementer following the AC would create a wrongly-named file that the `test_prompt_frontmatter` and `test_prompt_excludes_logs` tests (which load by id `ticket-digest` from stem `ticket_digest`) would not find.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria bullet 3 vs Scope section (`src/rebar/llm/reviewers/ticket_digest.md`) and What section (`reviewers/ticket_digest.md`)
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'The packaged `reviewers/enrich.md` prompt encodes...' with 'The packaged `reviewers/ticket_digest.md` prompt (id `ticket-digest`) encodes...' in AC bullet 3.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct AC #3 to reference `reviewers/ticket_digest.md` (not `reviewers/enrich.md`) to match the Scope and Testing sections.
findings.finding: AC item 3 names the prompt file as `reviewers/enrich.md`, but every other reference in the plan (Scope section, Testing section, other ACs) names it `reviewers/ticket_digest.md`. The file that `test_prompt_excludes_logs` and `test_prompt_frontmatter` load is `ticket_digest.md` (id `ticket-digest`), not `enrich.md`. An implementer following AC #3 literally would create the wrong file.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, item 3 ('The packaged `reviewers/enrich.md` prompt encodes...')
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC #3 to: 'The packaged `reviewers/ticket_digest.md` prompt (id `ticket-digest`) encodes the logs/stack-traces/code exclusion directive; `pytest tests/unit/test_enrich.py::test_prompt_excludes_logs`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix AC item 3 to name `reviewers/ticket_digest.md` (not `reviewers/enrich.md`) consistent with the Scope and Testing sections.
findings.finding: AC item 3 names the wrong prompt file: it says `reviewers/enrich.md` but every other section of the plan consistently names `reviewers/ticket_digest.md` (stem `ticket_digest` → id `ticket-digest`). This contradicts the Scope section and would cause `test_prompt_excludes_logs` to look for the wrong file.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, item 3: '- [ ] The packaged `reviewers/enrich.md` prompt encodes the logs/stack-traces/code exclusion directive'
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC item 3 to: 'The packaged `reviewers/ticket_digest.md` prompt encodes the logs/stack-traces/code exclusion directive; `pytest tests/unit/test_enrich.py::test_prompt_excludes_logs`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix AC line 3 to reference the correct prompt filename `reviewers/ticket_digest.md` (not `reviewers/enrich.md`) so it matches the file stem `ticket_digest` -> id `ticket-digest` used throughout the plan.
findings.finding: AC line 3 names the prompt file as `reviewers/enrich.md` but the plan names it `reviewers/ticket_digest.md` everywhere else (Scope section, system-prompt section, test section). An implementer following the AC would create the wrong file, and `test_prompt_excludes_logs` would either check the wrong path or fail to find the file.
findings.suggested_fix: Change AC line 3 from 'The packaged `reviewers/enrich.md` prompt encodes...' to 'The packaged `reviewers/ticket_digest.md` prompt encodes...'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct AC item 3 to name the prompt file consistently: replace `reviewers/enrich.md` with `reviewers/ticket_digest.md` (matching the Scope section's `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/ticket_digest.md` and the stem-to-id mapping `ticket_digest` -> `ticket-digest`).
findings.finding: The Acceptance Criteria section names the prompt file as `reviewers/enrich.md` but the Scope section specifies the file as `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/ticket_digest.md`. The AC proof command for `test_prompt_excludes_logs` is therefore internally inconsistent — an implementer following the AC literally could create a file named `enrich.md` that neither matches the stem-to-id mapping (`ticket_digest` -> `ticket-digest`) nor satisfies `test_prompt_frontmatter`'s `prompts.get_prompt("ticket-digest")` load.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria, item 3: 'The packaged `reviewers/enrich.md` prompt encodes the logs/stack-traces/code exclusion directive'
findings.suggested_fix:
Change the AC line to: 'The packaged `reviewers/ticket_digest.md` prompt (id `ticket-digest`) encodes the logs/stack-traces/code exclusion directive; `pytest tests/unit/test_enrich.py::test_prompt_excludes_logs`.'

Also ensure `test_prompt_excludes_logs` opens the file by resolved path via `prompts.get_prompt("ticket-digest")` (not a hardcoded `enrich.md` path) so the test is robust to the naming.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `ticket_id` → store-read path should use `rebar._reads.show_ticket(ticket_id, repo_root=...)` (as other LLM ops do), not the ambiguous `reads.show_state`; update the Op signature section accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan references `reads.show_state(ticket_id, ...)` without specifying which module `reads` is, and this symbol does not exist in the `rebar.llm` package. Existing LLM ops use `rebar._reads.show_ticket(ticket_id, repo_root=...)` (a public-facing wrapper). `show_state` lives in `rebar._engine_support.reads` — a lower-level private module that `rebar.llm` ops do not currently import directly.
findings.location: Op signature section: 'the op reads the ticket via `reads.show_state(ticket_id, ...)` and derives the source text with `gates._ticket_text(state)`'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add `rebar._engine_support.gates` to the Scope as an unenumerated consumer (noting the private import), or specify that `enrich.py` should inline the title+description+comments extraction rather than importing the private `gates._ticket_text`.
findings.finding: `gates._ticket_text` is a private symbol in `rebar._engine_support.gates` (not `rebar.llm.gates`). The plan names it as an import dependency of the new `enrich.py` op but does not list `rebar._engine_support.gates` in the edit targets or enumerate it as a new cross-package consumer. No existing `rebar.llm` module imports from `rebar._engine_support.gates`.
findings.location: Op signature section: 'derives the source text with `gates._ticket_text(state)` (title+description+comments)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact import path for `show_state` and the text-extraction call in `enrich.py`, or replace `gates._ticket_text` with an inline `title+description+comments` join (3 lines) or a call to the existing `rebar.llm.operations._format_ticket`, to avoid an undocumented cross-package private dependency.
findings.finding: The plan's `ticket_id` read path references `reads.show_state(ticket_id, ...)` and `gates._ticket_text(state)`, both of which are private functions in `rebar._engine_support` — outside `rebar.llm`. `_ticket_text` is underscore-prefixed (module-private). The plan does not specify the import path, leaving `enrich.py` with an undocumented cross-package private dependency that may break on refactor. A codebase-aligned alternative already exists: `_format_ticket` in `rebar.llm.operations` is public-within-the-module and produces analogous ticket text; or the op could inline the trivial title+description+comments join (3 lines) without depending on `_engine_support` internals at all.
findings.location: Op signature section — `ticket_id given -> the op reads the ticket via reads.show_state(ticket_id, ...) and derives the source text with gates._ticket_text(state)`
findings.suggested_fix: In `enrich.py`, inline the text extraction directly (it is 3 lines: title + description + comment bodies joined by newline) rather than importing `rebar._engine_support.gates._ticket_text`. For the store read, import `from rebar._engine_support.reads import show_state` and document this as the intentional cross-package seam — or expose a public `rebar.reads.show_state` wrapper consistent with the pattern in `rebar._reads.py:60`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace `reads.show_state(ticket_id, ...)` with `_reads.show_ticket(ticket_id, repo_root=cfg.repo_path)` (via `from rebar import _reads`, matching the pattern in `operations.py`) and inline the `_ticket_text` assembly (title + description + comments joined by newline) directly in `enrich.py` rather than importing `gates._ticket_text` from the private engine module.
findings.finding: The plan names 'reads.show_state(ticket_id, ...)' as the read call inside enrich.py, but no such function exists on the rebar.llm-accessible read surface. The internal function is rebar._engine_support.reads.show_state(ticket_id, tracker) — it requires a resolved tracker string, not a repo_root, and is never called directly from rebar.llm ops. Every existing rebar.llm operation uses 'from rebar import _reads' and calls '_reads.show_ticket(ticket_id, repo_root=...)' (the library facade). Implementing the plan as written would produce a NameError or import the wrong symbol.
findings.location: Op signature section: 'ticket_id given -> the op reads the ticket via reads.show_state(ticket_id, ...)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Op signature' section, replace: 'the op reads the ticket via reads.show_state(ticket_id, ...) and derives the source text with gates._ticket_text(state)' with: 'the op reads the ticket via `from rebar import _reads` then `state = _reads.show_ticket(ticket_id, repo_root=cfg.repo_path)`, and assembles the source text inline as title + description + comments joined by newlines (mirroring `_engine_support.gates._ticket_text` — a 5-line function not worth importing from a private engine module).'
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test assertion (e.g. in `test_propositions_bounded`) that passes an `LLMConfig` with non-default bounds (e.g. `overlap_propositions_max=4`) and verifies the op truncates at 4, confirming the op reads bounds from config rather than using hardcoded literals.
findings.finding: No AC or proving command verifies that `LLMConfig.overlap_propositions_min/max` are actually wired into the op's post-processing logic. `test_propositions_bounded` uses a FakeRunner with a pre-parsed structured result and asserts truncation/flag behaviour, but it cannot distinguish between the op reading bounds from `config.overlap_propositions_min/max` vs hardcoding literals `2` and `6`. An implementation that hardcodes the defaults would pass every test in the plan.
findings.location: Testing section: `test_propositions_bounded`; Scope section: `src/rebar/llm/config.py` config fields
findings.suggested_fix: In `test_propositions_bounded`, add a sub-case: construct `cfg = LLMConfig(..., overlap_propositions_max=4, overlap_propositions_min=2)`, seed FakeRunner with 5 propositions, call `enrich(text=..., config=cfg, runner=fake)`, and assert `len(result['digest']['propositions']) == 4` — proving the config value is read.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md section capturing the 'Cupid enrichment op' pattern: why `category: enrich` is distinct from `category: review`, the co-location contract-registration convention, and the `text=` injection seam design.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new 'Cupid enrichment op' architectural pattern (a distinct 'enrich' category separate from 'review', a new op shape, a new prompt category, and a new schema wiring convention) but does not include an ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc update to capture this decision for future maintainers.
findings.location: Scope section / overall plan — no ADR/doc update mentioned
findings.suggested_fix: Add a scope item: 'Update `docs/adr/` or `CLAUDE.md` with an "Enrich Ops" section covering: (1) Cupid pattern rationale, (2) `category: enrich` vs `category: review` prompt distinction, (3) builder co-location in `contracts.py`, (4) `text=` injection seam as the preferred test isolation mechanism.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state whether the runner framework provides retry/backoff for transient LLM errors (rate-limit, timeout), and if not, add a note on how callers of `enrich()` should handle retriable failures.
findings.finding: The plan does not address retry/backoff behavior for transient LLM failures (rate-limit 429s, network timeouts) during `get_runner(cfg).run(req)`. It is unspecified whether the runner framework already provides retry logic or whether the enrich op is exposed to bare transient failures.
findings.location: Error contract section / Op signature section

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a plan-level statement specifying what the op logs on error (e.g. ticket_id, error type, trace_id) and when `low_proposition_count=True` is set, so operators have actionable signals without code archaeology.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify any observability instrumentation (log line, metric, or trace annotation) for enrich op failures. The return value includes a `trace_id` from the runner but there is no plan-level specification for what is logged when the op raises or when `low_proposition_count=True` is flagged.
findings.location: Error contract section / Return shape section
findings.suggested_fix: In `enrich.py`, emit a structured log line (e.g. `logger.warning('low_proposition_count', ticket_id=ticket_id, count=len(propositions), trace_id=run_result.get('trace_id'))`) and on exception re-raise after logging the ticket_id and trace_id.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the two `LLMConfig` fields (`overlap_propositions_min/max`) and their env vars with module-level constants in `enrich.py` (e.g. `_PROPOSITIONS_MIN = 2` / `_PROPOSITIONS_MAX = 6`), unless a current AC or sibling story explicitly requires runtime tunability via the config layer.
findings.finding: The plan adds two new LLMConfig dataclass fields (overlap_propositions_min/overlap_propositions_max) and two env vars (REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_PROPOSITIONS_MIN/MAX) for bounds that are (a) already expressed as literals in the prompt text, (b) consumed only by the single enrich op, and (c) backed by no evidence of a runtime-tuning requirement. All three existing _llm_int call-sites (max_tokens, max_iterations, timeout_s) are framework-wide knobs consumed by every op and the runner; the proposed fields have exactly one consumer. This is config-surface proliferation without the Rule-of-Three evidence base required at the LLMConfig layer.
findings.location: Scope section: `src/rebar/llm/config.py` edits; Config-bound threading section
findings.suggested_fix: In enrich.py: `_PROPOSITIONS_MIN = 2; _PROPOSITIONS_MAX = 6` and reference them directly. Remove the LLMConfig fields, their DEFAULT_* module constants from config.py, and the two _llm_int lines from from_env. If future stories need tunability, add the fields then with the Rule-of-Three satisfied.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
